---
# =====================================================================
#  Research highlight v2 — streamlined one-pager
#  Render:  quarto render epa-compliance-workshops_research-highlight_v2.qmd
#  Design system: _style.tex (colours, fonts, boxes, macros)
# =====================================================================
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: pdflatex
    documentclass: article
    fontsize: 11pt
    geometry:
      - top=0.38in
      - bottom=0.38in
      - left=0.65in
      - right=0.65in
    include-in-header: ../../../../formats/research-highlight/_style.tex
    keep-tex: true
---

```{=latex}
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% MASTHEAD
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\briefheader{%
  \includegraphics[height=0.45in]{../../../../formats/logos/eil-logo-maroon.png}%
}{Research Highlight}

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% TITLE + CITATION
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\brieftitle{EPA Compliance Workshops Don't Reduce Violations}
\briefcitation{Paul J. Ferraro \& Jay P. Shimshack \textperiodcentered{} \textit{Journal of Policy Analysis and Management}, 2026 \textperiodcentered{} \href{https://doi.org/10.1002/pam.70056}{doi.org/10.1002/pam.70056}}

\vspace{6pt}

% override body font size for this document
\fontsize{10.5pt}{14pt}\selectfont

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% BOTTOM LINE
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\begin{bottomline}
{\fontsize{11pt}{15pt}\selectfont
\textbf{Offering polluters free help to follow environmental rules does not make them more likely to follow them.} Researchers studied thousands of facilities that received EPA compliance workshops and found no reduction in violations --- the program looked effective at first glance, but the improvement was a pre-existing trend, not caused by the workshops.
}
\end{bottomline}

\vspace{8pt}

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% BACKGROUND
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\sectionhead{Background}
\noindent When a polluting facility breaks environmental rules, the EPA has two main options: levy a fine, or offer a helping hand. For the past decade, the agency has been quietly shifting from the first to the second --- cutting back on inspections and penalties in favor of free \textbf{compliance assistance}: workshops and consulting designed to help facilities understand and follow the rules. The logic is appealing. But this study asks a question that had never been rigorously tested: does it actually work?

\vspace{8pt}

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% FIGURE — centred, stand-alone
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\begin{center}
\begin{minipage}{0.72\linewidth}
\figtitle{Violations per facility, 2014--2019}{Source: Ferraro \& Shimshack (2026).}
\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{../../data-viz/figures/fig1-v2.png}
\vspace{1pt}
{\color{muted}\fontsize{7.6pt}{10.5pt}\selectfont\textit{Violations were already falling before the EPA's workshops started --- and kept falling at exactly the same rate afterward. The black line shows what actually happened; the dashed line shows the trend that was already in motion beforehand. The two never separate. If the workshops worked, the black line would have dropped below the dashed one.}\par}
\end{minipage}
\end{center}

\vspace{10pt}

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% TAKEAWAY
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\sectionhead{Takeaway}
\noindent Before trading a proven enforcement tool for a newer alternative, \impllink{test whether the alternative actually works}. Inspections and penalties have a strong track record of reducing violations; compliance assistance does not. With EPA enforcement resources under pressure, scaling an unproven program comes at a real cost. The EPA has the data to run this kind of evaluation --- this study is a model for how it should be done, and a reminder that good intentions are not a substitute for evidence.

\vspace{2pt}

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% FOOTER
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\brieffooter{Method: pre-registered difference-in-differences comparing assisted facilities to those that declined assistance, using monthly facility-level violation records.}
```
